The gap between the rhetoric and the reality of deportation is wide and the people paying the price. For the clearest data-driven coverage of what's actually happening, follow: austinkocher.substack.com

The pressure to produce numbers has changed how the system works: by November 2025, for every one person released from ICE detention pending a hearing, 14.3 were deported directly from custody compared to a roughly one-to-two ratio the year before.

Interior deportations rose 4.6 times between the second half of 2024 and September/October 2025. But even at that accelerated pace, the administration is on track for roughly 300,000 removals unprecedented, but far short of the million-per-year target they've been claiming.

At-large arrests agents sweeping communities rather than executing targeted warrants increased by 600%. This is how you end up detaining a 19-year-old University of Utah student on her way to work and a U.S. citizen walking to lunch.

Despite claiming to target the "worst of the worst," the data shows the opposite. ICE increased arrests of people with no criminal record by 2,450% in Trump's first year the most dramatic shift in enforcement profile in the agency's history.

In Minnesota, the ACLU sued after a 20-year-old U.S. citizen named Mubashir Hussen was stopped by masked ICE agents while walking to lunch. He repeatedly told them he was a citizen. They shackled him, took his fingerprints, and only released him after he showed his passport.

ICE kept making those arrests anyway. By February 2026, the ACLU filed a 98-page amended complaint alleging agents were still targeting people based on the color of their skin. Attorneys say ICE "has continued to thumb their nose both at the law and court orders."
